data.title: The Only 4 Investments I Will Ever Need
data.description:
In this article I will detail the only four income investments that I need to have a sufficiently diversified portfolio that will provide me with reasonably stable performance through all sorts of interest rate and economic cycles while also paying me attractive passive income and enjoying exposure to the AI revolution.

Why I Practice Value Dividend Investing

I am an income- and value-focused investor for one big reason. I believe that true investing is trying to buy pieces of businesses and assets for less than they are intrinsically worth because all other forms of investing are effectively speculation because they're simply betting on the fact that a certain pattern of either human behavior or stock chart activity will continue long enough into the future that I will be able to make a profit off the trade and ultimately also be able to interpret when to stop following that trend.

Instead, when engaging in value investing, I am trying to buy a quality business at a good price, and if I can do that well or at least often enough and diversify intelligently enough, I can then rest easy knowing that time is on my side and that eventually, in the long run, the market will move from being a voting machine to a weighing machine, and I will end up achieving solid returns. Sometimes this happens as quickly as one week, and sometimes this takes years or even a decade to fully manifest itself.

Of course, this all rests on the notion that I can properly value a business often enough such that I do not overpay for companies too often, which in turn would completely undermine my approach and likely lead to very disappointing total returns over time.

Why Dividends Simplify Valuation And Boost Discipline

This is why I focus so much on income investing because stocks that generate a high percentage of their return from dividends that are backed by stable, defensive, and durable cash flows are often much easier to value since the growth part of the equation, which is much harder to predict over long periods of time, is a much less prominent part of the equation.

In particular, when I target investments that pay out mid-single digit to low double-digit yields, very little growth is required beyond inflationary increases and perhaps one or two hundred basis points beyond that through cash flow and other organic growth initiatives to achieve a 10 to 12 percent annualized total return. I can then overlay an opportunistic capital recycling program by selling stocks once they reach close to my fair value estimate and reapplying the proceeds into significantly undervalued assets and then holding these stocks in my Roths to be able to mitigate tax consequences to turn 10 to 12 percent annualized total returns into 15 to 20 percent annualized total returns, thereby not only handling meeting the broader high yield in dividend stock sectors but even meeting the S&P 500 over the long run.

This has been my performance so far across over 15 years of investing, and I'm optimistic that it will continue into the future. However, when focusing on the income space becomes more important than ever to make sure that you are properly diversified and in particular, diversified across various macro environments to avoid getting plunged into long periods of excessively weak performance due to macro headwinds.

Diversifying Across Macro Environments

A clear recent example of this is the REIT sector (VNQ) which has had very poor performance ever since the Fed began hiking interest rates aggressively in early 2022:

Additionally, BDCs (BIZD) are often vulnerable during economic downturns because that is a period in which the Fed typically cuts rates aggressively, which weighs on their profit margins since they primarily invest in floating rate loans while also weakening their underwriting performance and thereby their market sentiment because of weakening economic conditions leading to a spike in non-accruals and their investment portfolios. This compounds further due to the fact that they are typically fairly heavily leveraged instruments (which accelerates reductions in NAV whenever loans become distressed), and weakening market sentiment pushes their price to NAV ratios lower.

Finally, you also can see the precious metal space being particularly streaky, as gold (GLD) had an incredible bull run on the tail end of the Great Recession only to be followed by a lost decade, which has since given birth to an epic bull run for both gold and silver (SLV) and especially gold mining stocks (GDX) over the past few years.

Therefore, if you go all in on gold at the wrong time, you can experience massive losses in a short period of time followed by a long period of little to no return performance, and as you lose heart and sell at the wrong time, you can miss out on spectacular returns in a very short period of time.

This all goes back to my point about being careful about diversification, and with that in mind, in the rest of this article I am going to share the only four investments that I need to be able to pursue my aforementioned investment strategy to achieve adequate diversification across macro sectors.

To achieve that balance, I’ve distilled my portfolio into four core investment categories: precious metals, REITs, BDCs, and infrastructure (UTF). Together, they provide complementary performance profiles that provide fairly stable performance across different macro backdrops.

1. Precious Metals: Gold Bullion Leases As Portfolio Insurance

The first one I will mention is gold bullion leases. This is because they provide a natural hedge against debasement, de-dollarization, and geopolitical risks that are running rampant right now as central banks are increasingly moving away from the dollar in favor of gold, runaway deficit spending in the U.S. is sparking growing concerns of monetary debasement, and simmering tensions between the U.S. and China are causing growing concerns of a potential conflict over Taiwan. Such a conflict could devastate the global economy, threaten to unseat the U.S. dollar as the global reserve currency, and would likely send gold soaring while the rest of the stock market and global economy would tumble.

This makes gold an essential part of your portfolio, especially today, given how pronounced these various macro risks are. In fact, many ask me if I think gold is worth buying today even after its epic bull run. My response is typically, if you do not own any or very much, yes, because it is such an essential piece of portfolio insurance, especially if macro trends like de-dollarization and geopolitical risks accelerate to result in a global depression and or an unseating of the dollar. However, if you already have a large position, it may not necessarily be prudent to buy more unless you are completely convinced that these macro trends are unstoppable and likely to accelerate in the not-too-distant future.

2. Real Estate Investment Trusts: Leveraged Inflation Hedges

Another investment that I would want to have is REITs. The reason I like REITs so much is that in scenarios in which long-term interest rates fall, REITs are a great bet on this, similar to what bonds (BND) are. However, REITs have several advantages over bonds. Namely, they are leveraged, so they tend to experience greater upside when long-term interest rates fall. Two, they are real assets, so over the long term, they are much better inflation hedges since the value of the underlying real estate and the cash flows that they can generate tend to increase with inflation on average over the long term.

Additionally, through active management and retained cash flows, they can generate additional growth beyond inflation, leading to attractive inflation-beating dividend growth and stock price growth over the long term, helping to not only preserve but grow passive income investors' standard of living from cash flows as well as their underlying net worth. Finally, thanks to leverage and a risk premium, they often offer significantly higher total return potential and even yield potential in some cases relative to long-term investment-grade bonds, making options like Realty Income (O), W. P. Carey (WPC), and even Easterly Government Properties (DEA) attractive income picks in the current environment.

3. Business Development Companies: Counterweights To Gold And REITs

A third investment that I would want to have is BDCs. These provide a nice counterweight to the other two investments so far listed in this article. This is because gold tends to soar during periods of geopolitical risk. Additionally, falling short-term interest rates make gold an attractive alternative to cash as a store of value since the opportunity cost of holding bullion relative to cash in a CD or other savings or short-term treasuries is a more attractive option, especially when you consider gold leases that also pay yields on fairly short-term durations.

In contrast, when interest rates rise, gold tends to suffer more; however, BDCs are the opposite, as they tend to rise when short-term interest rates rise because they invest primarily in loans that are indexed to short-term interest rates. While the debt they take out is typically a mix of floating rate and fixed rate debt, even in the cases where it is all floating rate debt on the liabilities side of a BDC's balance sheet, they still tend to have roughly two times as many loans on the asset side of their balance sheet as they do on the liabilities side of the balance sheet. Therefore, rising short-term interest rates in those cases still lead to rising net investment income for them. This can lead to rising dividends and more retained cash flow, which can lead to higher NAVs and in particular higher price-to-NAV multiples as investors pile into the sector in chase of the higher yields being offered.

Of course, when interest rates fall, that means that their net investment income per share falls, and that can lead to dividend cuts if the interest rate reductions are particularly steep. Additionally, when the economy turns south and/or the markets move to a risk-off environment, that can cause non-accruals to rise and at a bare minimum, the price-to-NAV multiples to shrink, leading to poor total return on performance for BDCs.

4. Infrastructure: The All-Weather Income Engine

Last but not least, infrastructure is the fourth and perhaps my favorite investment of these four that I want to have in my portfolio. This is because infrastructure combines the long-duration bond-like cash flows that REITs enjoy with other attractive mechanisms, including often having inflation indexation in the contracts as is typically seen in many midstream companies (AMLP) like Enbridge (ENB) and Energy Transfer (ET) as well as in broader infrastructure plays like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BEPC).

Additionally, infrastructure can give investors exposure to the AI boom that is largely missed in the other three investments discussed in this article. This is important to have in one's portfolio given that AI has the potential to become a completely dominant and transformative technology for not only the economy but society as a whole in the coming decades. Therefore, I think it is important to have at least some exposure to it in your portfolio. With infrastructure, you can get AI exposure through utilities (UTG) and other power generation companies that are used to fuel data centers or you can invest in midstream infrastructure, and in particular natural gas infrastructure through companies like Williams Companies (WMB), that will also be useful in helping to power data centers. Finally, you can even invest in data center infrastructure itself through companies like BIP and Blue Owl Capital (OWL) which manages a major data center developer and owner that leases it to hyperscalers like Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT).

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, value investing with high-yielding stocks can be a great way to compound wealth and passive income over time due to the generally simpler nature of valuing stocks that are high-yielding and slow in growth rather than trying to figure out what extremely richly valued and high-growing stocks like Palantir (PLTR) are really going to be worth and how much they are going to be able to sustain or even accelerate their growth 5, 10, 15 years in the future which is needed to be able to make sense of their current lofty valuations.

Of course, taking this approach requires extra care to ensure that you are achieving proper diversification. This is why I think the four investment classes mentioned in this article, namely precious metals (in particular, bullion leases since gold bullion over the long term has significantly outperformed gold miners, making it generally a better long-term buy and hold investment while the yield offered by leases in the current environment of about 3-4% are significantly higher than the 1-2% yields typically offered by gold miners today), REITs, BDCs, and infrastructure are so important.

Additionally, REITs and BDCs balance each other out pretty well when it comes to interest rate volatility. Finally infrastructure businesses, especially ones with strong balance sheets, tend to be quite defensive but also quite capable of playing offense and thereby suitable for all sorts of economic environments due to their inflation indexation while also being well-positioned in many cases to enjoy substantial tailwinds from the ongoing AI boom, making them an essential component of a value-oriented dividend investor's portfolio. As such, these are my four primary sectors to allocate capital to in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Applied Digital: I See No Froth
data.description:
I personally find it surprising that I haven't initiated coverage on Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ:APLD) thus far. The company has become a big name in the world of data centers, and as you can see below, the stock has seen a meteoric rise in the past few months. 52-week gains stand at a whopping 382% as of now, and from this performance, it can be inferred that demand for AI compute is relentless.

In the below analysis, it is shown that demand is solid. Revenue growth accelerated, and more importantly, Polaris Forge 1 is now fully leased out. With Polaris Forge 2 breaking ground, the company is set for $1 billion in NOI by 2030, and so the valuation is actually much cheaper than what multiples currently imply. With demand being robust and Applied Digital being aggressive with their expansion, I would say the stock still has room for outperformance moving forward. Therefore, I have decided to initiate with a buy rating.

Demand Is Robust

Applied Digital reported their FY2026 Q1 earnings just a few days ago on Oct 9th, and it seems that demand is truly solid. The company generated a total of $64.2 million in revenues, representing an 84% increase over the prior year period. This is compared with a 41% growth rate back in FY2025 Q4 and so at this point, it seems that demand is actually accelerating and strengthening. They also crushed revenue expectations as they beat them by $18.75 million. For a company of their size, that's major outperformance, and so demand is stronger than anticipated. While some may worry about excess capacity, at this point, Applied Digital may actually be constrained by supply.

Polaris Forge 1

North Dakota

As a result of a major strategic pivot a few years ago, HPC Hosting is now Applied Digital's most important business. Moving forward, this segment will likely be their core growth driver. As of now, the major focus of the company is on Polaris Forge 1. Taking a step back, I would say that Applied Digital's decision to be stationed in North Dakota is a smart one. While they disclose many good reasons for building their data centers there, I believe two are standouts.

Firstly, the state has plenty of energy. By now, it is no secret that energy is a bottleneck for AI compute. Applied Digital stated that "North Dakota generated 50% more electricity than it used in 2023, producing 42 million MWh vs 28 million MWh consumed." Clearly, data centers there will not suffer from a lack of energy. In fact, due to this abundance of energy, North Dakota has electricity rates around 24% below the national average. This will be beneficial for their margins, and those effects should trickle down to shareholders.

The second major reason why North Dakota is a prime choice is because of the climate. The state experiences overall colder weather. The company stated that "North Dakota’s cold weather results in over 200 days a year of free cooling." Bleeding-edge AI infrastructure currently needs liquid cooling due to the massive energy used. Since Applied Digital will be able to operate most of the year without cooling, this should help to boost their margins as well.

In fact, in total, the company expects that the choice of operating in North Dakota will reduce annual operating costs by $50 million to $60 million for a 100MW data center. This will obviously benefit the bottom line and thus shareholders in the long run. Management deserves credit for their strategic decision to base their operations in a very favorable part of the country.

Expansion

As another sign of solid demand, the Polaris Forge 1 campus is now fully leased. In their business highlights for Q1, Applied Digital said that they finalized a new lease with CoreWeave (CRWV) for another 150MW. At this point, over the next 15 years, this campus is expected to bring in around $11 billion in revenue and so the future looks bright for the company. As you can see in the diagram above, building 1 is very near completion, and they already initiated their tenant fit-out for CoreWeave. Construction has reportedly begun on their building 2 that will be completed before the middle of next year. With this campus being now fully leased, the company will quickly feel the financial benefits as these buildings become operational.

Polaris Forge 2

While Polaris Forge 1 is taking a lot of the buzz currently, the company is already working on Polaris Forge 2. Applied Digital has reported that they have broken ground. They plan for 2 initial 150MW buildings but this campus may scale to 1GW in the future. This capacity is very unlikely to be unused as they are in "advanced discussions" with a hyperscaler to lease out the space at this new campus. This clearly indicates that as of now, Applied Digital's growth is likely constrained by capacity rather than demand. That is a great problem to have. Again, as the company continues to build out their campuses and expand, investors can expect the financial benefits to come rather quickly. Judging from what we're seeing with both Polaris Forge 1 and 2, I would say that the company's rather aggressive move to scale is very likely to pay off.

Financial Standing

For the bottom line, Applied Digital narrowed their loss significantly YoY. They reported an adjusted net loss of $7.6 million versus the year-ago quarter's $21.6 million, and so the company is getting closer to being profitable. However, as you can see above, the cash burn of the company is significant.

Applied Digital used $82.02 million for operations in Q1, and after including the significant CAPEX needed to fund expansion, free cash flow comes out to be -$331.44 million. At the end of August, the company's cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash totaled $114.1 million. While that may seem concerning, I would say that their ability to fund their projects is still strong.

They have a $5 billion preferred equity facility with Macquarie Asset Management, and in Q1, Applied Digital used $112.5 million to fund Polaris Forge 1. They were also able to get $50 million in funding for their Polaris Forge 2 from Macquarie Equipment Capital, and so I would say that their financing options are quite extensive, and investors should not overly worry about a cash crunch currently.

Valuation Lower Than It Seems

The above chart looks quite terrifying. Since July, the forward P/S ratio has exploded upwards, and according to Seeking Alpha, the stock trades at a ~903% premium to the IT sector according to this measure. However, the valuation is really not as absurd as it seems.

In their earnings, Applied Digital did not provide near-term financial guidance, but their longer term targets are encouraging. They predict that when Polaris Forge 1 is fully operational, they will have an annualized NOI run rate of around $500 million. With Polaris Forge 2 also coming up, they project $1 billion in NOI within five years.

As of this writing, Applied Digital's market cap is at $10.56 billion, and so when you consider the highly strong long-term outlook, the valuation is actually very reasonable and perhaps even cheap. With the trajectory of demand continuing to be strong and the company aggressively expanding, I would say that they are well-positioned to hit these targets, and thus, current levels in the stock likely still provide significant opportunity.

Conclusion

Applied Digital successfully shifted the company's positioning a few years ago, and now they are set to become a key part of the AI picture. With spending by hyperscalers showing no signs of slowing down, Applied Digital's growth is really just constrained by capacity. They are clearly focused on addressing that with ambitious plans for Polaris Forge 2. The stock's valuation multiple can easily scare off investors, but the valuation is much more reasonable than you would expect. The company trades at just over 10x 2030 NOI, and so the risk/reward setup is very favorable in my view. As a result, I'm not hesitating to call Applied Digital a buy.

data.title: TSMC: The AI Bottleneck
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (NYSE:TSM)(OTC:TSMWF) was in April 2025, when I reluctantly called it a buy amidst the tariff chaos, which didn’t seem to daunt its customers at all (emphasis mine):

The key takeaway, to me, remains the immense strength of the AI trend. Even the incredible chaos introduced by the White House’s trade policy, doesn’t seem able to derail it.

Given TSM’s commitment to production in Arizona, and its critical role within the strategic AI industry, I expect it will be able to deal with further industry-specific tariffs. I also expect the administration likely wants to preserve a vibrant AI industry to stay ahead of China.

The stock is up 86% since. I was specifically focused on CapEx, as an adjustment could betray management’s true view of demand.

These days, management has significantly increased CapEx, and I’m afraid it isn’t enough. It wouldn’t surprise me at all if TSM becomes, if it isn’t already, a bottleneck in the AI supply chain.

It’s very understandable because this is a very capital intensive layer. TSM buys its tools from a variety of equipment suppliers like ASML Holding (ASML), Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research Corporation (LRCX), KLA Corporation (KLAC) and many others. Just ASML's bleeding-edge machines to work below 3nm are between $200 and $400 million a piece. These machines tend to make a lot of money for 5-7 years. After that, they remain in use for 23-25 more years to produce less advanced chips.

If you are in a capital-intensive business, a boom can turn out really bad for shareholders. As the boom is ongoing, everyone is pushing you to deliver more capacity and willing to pay high prices. But building a new fab takes 2-3 years. Ordering and receiving a new fleet of tankers or containerships (typical capital intensive business) can take even longer. As the boom is ongoing, you are investing, construction costs are high, and everything is at its most expensive because your competitors are buying as well. At some point it ends.

The economy cools, demand normalizes, or a software design innovation decreases the demand for compute. The new, multi-billion dollar factory finally comes online. As well as the capacity your competitor has been building out. The industry is now flooded with excess capacity, and you end up taking huge losses for many years.

This is a typical pattern for businesses without competitive advantages in capital-intensive cyclical industries. The semiconductor industry is rife with examples of companies that failed exactly because of this dynamic.

Ultimately, this got “solved” by companies moving to a fabless model while TSM specialized as a fab. Two players tried to keep up with an integrated model; Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Intel (INTC). An industry with three fabs is much better (from a fab’s shareholder perspective) than an industry full of IDMs. TSM ended up being the only game in town on the bleeding edge. That’s obviously a great position. It also means it can avoid the boom-bust phenomenon much more easily.

Why can it ignore the boom-bust of the capital-intensive industry? Because it isn’t forced to invest during boom times. In a competitive industry, this would lead to market share losses and competitors enjoying longer and more boom profits. To an extent, it can sit back and enjoy the boom. When the bust comes, TSM is still the only game in town on the bleeding edge and doesn’t have to lower prices. Its negotiation position is much stronger during both parts of the cycle.

I looked up a few titles (of well-known publications) that are exemplary of a theme I see everywhere:

“Fears over AI bubble bursting grow in Silicon Valley.” Stocks may be in an AI bubble. Is it time to hoard cash? "Absolutely’ a market bubble: Wall Street sounds the alarm on AI‑driven boom"

In my opinion these pieces are mostly ill-informed. I think you can make a credible argument, though I wouldn’t necessarily agree, for Nvidia (NVDA) being overvalued or OpenAI (OPENAI) or Anthropic or whatever. There are many companies that aren't overvalued, and there is a real risk we're underinvesting (in the right things).

But if you were the CEO of a capital-intensive company in a cyclical business, would you be dying to expand capacity?

In my article on ASML from a few days ago, I wrote:

I think we're currently experiencing chip production capacity issues. Probably because inference is growing faster than anticipated. It is possible this is (in-part) caused by networking, memory, and other ancillary issues. There's no use in TSMC scaling up GPU production if these can't be supported.

TSM and ASML are critical to the supply of compute in the coming years. If hyperscalers and model companies want to achieve their ambitions, the current investment trajectory by the latter isn’t cutting it. TSM’s investments are a signal to ASML that also needs time to scale up its output. Keep in mind that a dollar of announced CapEx at the earliest results in capacity coming online 1.5 - 2 years later.

One of the most interesting pieces of information from TSM’s call is the following:

We give you a number, roughly 40 in the mid-40s is a CAGR, not including all the infrastructures built up and also aligned with our major customers' forecasts or their view. More than that, I think if we are talking about the tokens, the number of tokens increase, it's exponential. I believe that almost every three months, it will be exponentially increased. That's why we are still very comfortable that the demand on leading-edge semiconductors is real. As I continue to say, we look at all the demand and look at our capacity expansion, we need TSMC to work very hard to narrow the gap. That's what we are doing right now.

This sort of lines up with what Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) has been saying on its earnings calls. Here’s a chart by Stephen Smith:

Illustrating the growth rate; token growth rates of other companies are a bit harder to track. OpenAI is definitely growing fast as well with the recent Sora2 release and given its revenue growth rate (especially in light of token generation declining in price by 10x per year):

The growth rates of all these companies are remarkable. It is one reason why I’m not as skeptical about the OpenAI commitments to purchase compute as is common these days.

To get everyone in the industry to align and work together to scale compute supply, I believe the companies with the real demand insight, the model providers, are sharing their ground-level data about the growth they’re experiencing. Almost all of them are limiting the consumption of compute because they don’t have enough. I think Alphabet, apparently one of the fastest growing, has the lowest cost base, because of its massive TPU fleet, and is the least constrained.

This compute constraint is also limiting the improvements that can be incorporated and rolled out to customers. An analyst questions whether their CapEx is sufficient to serve the growth rates:

Okay. Okay, Bruce. You are right. You are right. The tokens and the number of tokens that increase exponentially is much, much higher than TSMC's CAGR as we forecasted. Let me tell you that first, our technology continues to improve. Our customer is moving from one node to the next node so that they can handle much more tokens' number in their basic fundamental calculation. That's one thing. You know we progress very well from one node to the other node. Our customer is working with TSMC to continuously improve their performance. That's why when we say that we have about 40, 45% CAGR, the token number exponentially increases because of our customer and TSMC's technology combined that can handle much more or much efficiently than before.

Let’s assume for a second that if TSM increases its AI revenue by 40%-45% on the AI side, this increases its output of compute by more than 40%-45%. That’s very likely, although the U.S. facilities are a bit less efficient. They also state that their customer is moving to another node. This is likely Nvidia moving to 2nm (which is supposed to ship in March 2027). AMD (AMD) is moving to 2nm a little bit earlier, but they don’t have the scale to make a real dent in the market (yet). The new node is at a newer manufacturing process. Most likely production won’t be at the exact level of smoothness as the 3nm process.

However, GPU efficiency will be better at 2nm vs. 3nm meaning more compute output per chip or unit of power. Between 3nm and 2nm improvements in compute per chip could be in the 15%-30% range. Improvements in the AI factory (memory, bandwidth, cooling, etc.) can add to that. But we're nowhere near matching the level of token growth the CEO indicated.

One of the most important ways to increase efficiency per token is at the software level, and I think companies are getting increasingly interested in optimizing there. The level of investments required is so high that it is starting to matter more and more.

At first capturing customers was the priority, but at scale efficiency is starting to become very important. If anything, to demonstrate to investors or capital markets the long-term margins will be really attractive. OpenAI may have to IPO near the end of 2026 or 2027 (and demonstrate a credible path to future attractive economics) if it wants to keep up with Alphabet.

It is very hard to believe ~40% output growth in compute is going to deal with token demand that’s doubling every 3 months… That’s while the token demand is actively slowed down because of existing supply issues.

I think this industry dynamic is the reason why we’re seeing these odd circular deals. TSM can mitigate the investment risk that’s offloaded to them by demanding long-term offtake contracts from companies. That shifts the risk to Broadcom (AVGO), AMD, and Nvidia.

It is more challenging for them to recycle this risk. Alphabet is a great customer to Broadcom, but obviously with OpenAI or Anthropic, notwithstanding growth rates, there is a chance they’re not good for the money.

The OpenAI/AMD deal is a specific case that I’ve discussed in: Why A Bad Deal Makes AMD A Strong Buy. In most cases I view this as Oracle (ORCL) or Nvidia signaling to TSM and others in the supply chain they are buying into this growth. Capital markets closing, transformer models plateauing, or whatever could temporarily slow growth down. They're showing they don't think this uptick in compute demand is a fad, and they don't want the growth to get impaired.

A credible reason for TSM to be prudent about scaling up is that it believes in the demand numbers but also sees that it isn’t the only bottleneck. Power is often mentioned as a constraint as well. It isn’t fun if you invest billions of dollars in supply and it turns out there simply isn’t enough power for your customers to turn on their stuff. I’m going to examine the power issue in an in-depth article of its own.

This has been a long-winded way of arguing that I believe TSM is holding back.

Its projected growth rates for AI-demand of 40%+ are very realistic. I think it could exceed these projections or start making additional investments if some of its concerns are adequately addressed.

From the behavior in the industry so far, it appears to me many parties are interested in working together to address bottlenecks. These bottlenecks will keep coming up. The demand growth is so high it makes total sense the industry runs into bottleneck after bottleneck. TSM may simply believe it isn't the bottleneck at this level of investment.

In terms of valuation, the company trades at 31x earnings, an EV/EBITDA of 14x (you could view earnings as depressed given the high level of growth CapEx) and 16x free cash flow. It doesn’t look so bad compared to its Seeking Alpha peer group:

The S&P 500 (SPY) trades at ~30x earnings. Is TSM more attractive than the average S&P 500 company? It says it will now grow in the mid-30% for the full 2025. Around 57% of its revenue is high-performance compute (mostly AI) related, about which it says (emphasis mine):

So the CAGR we -- previously we announced is about mid-40s. It's still -- it's a little bit better than that. We will update you probably in beginning of next year. So we have a more clear picture. Today, the number are insane.

Prodded by analysts, the CEO confirms this number holds with China being restricted and discouraging purchases from U.S. companies in return. These projections also don’t include adjustments for on-edge AI. I think on-edge AI is 1-2 years away, but it is very likely it will give a real boost to the smartphone and PC markets. Look at these growth numbers for revenue as well as profitability:

Sure there are risks. There is a lot of tension between the U.S. and China. Sometimes it is argued China may want to invade Taiwan. That would be bad. You could consider mitigating this by buying deep out-of-the-money puts. The geographic diversification efforts (under pressure from the U.S.) also help.

Another, more pedestrian, risk is capital markets close-up. Model companies would be challenged to sustain their growth rates. It wouldn’t be disastrous for TSM, but no doubt its growth would slow down. There is some turmoil in private credit markets, so it isn't inconceivable.

At the end of the day, this is the only company currently able to deliver chips at scale on the bleeding edge. It is investing at an unprecedented rate. Which I believe falls far short of what’s needed to actually fill its customers demand. TSM looks like a great buy to me. I also think there’s a very important read-through for ASML in that TSM’s main customer is moving to 2nm. This requires ASML's more expensive machines.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: India Gold Market Update: Festive Shine
data.description:
Highlights

  • Gold price rally intensifies and domestic premium climbs
  • The festive season starts strongly, powered by investment demand and jewellery sales
  • September sees record inflows into gold ETFs and the momentum carries forward
  • Listed jewellers report strong quarterly revenue gains despite the high gold price and base effect
  • Gold imports hit a 10-month high in August, supported by seasonal buying and investment demand.

Looking ahead

  • Expectations for a strong festive season remain high across the gold trade, driven by continued demand for investment products and wedding jewellery, despite challenges in the broader jewellery market amidst a rising gold price.
  • Increased disposable income for consumers – following GST cuts and the prevailing low inflation environment1 – may give gold a boost.

Fresh record for gold

Gold continues its extraordinary price rally, having reached 48 all-time highs this year.2 Geopolitical tensions, strong investment demand, dollar weakness, financial market risk and expectations of dovish Fed policy lifted international prices by 11.6% in September and by an additional 7.9% in the first two weeks of October, pushing past the US$4,000/oz milestone. Notably, the surge from $3,500 to $4,000 occurred in just 36 days, a stark contrast to the previous $500/oz increments, which took over three years on average. International gold prices have surged by 58% y-t-d, marking the largest increase in 45 years. Domestically, gold prices have mirrored this upward trend, rising 66% y-t-d, with gains further amplified by weakness in the Indian rupee.

Chart 1: The gold rally continues in both international and domestic markets

End-of-month LBMA Price PM and MCX domestic spot price levels and m/m changes *

Since mid-September, domestic gold prices have been trading at a sustained premium over international prices (Chart 2). As of mid-October, the premium has risen to US$25/oz,3 marking the highest level since July 2024, when the customs duty cut boosted the spread. This move is indicative of the domestic demand conditions detailed below.

Chart 2: The domestic gold price premium has climbed as the festive season kicked off

NCDEX gold premium/discount relative to the international price*

Record prices fuel festive sales and investment interest

The festive season has begun on a positive note, with market feedback and anecdotal evidence indicating healthy demand for both physical investment products and jewellery. The record rally in the gold price has drawn investor attention and fuelled sustained interest in physical gold. As a result, the share of investment demand within overall domestic gold consumption is on the rise.4 Despite the pressure on gold jewellery consumption due to high prices and affordability, there has been a recent sales uptick, predominantly concentrated around wedding-related purchases and aligning with the wedding season. High prices are prompting consumers to opt for lighter-weight, lower-carat pieces, affecting overall retail volumes. While retailers’ revenues may benefit from a boost in jewellery sales, overall volume demand is bound to decline due to affordability constraints from high gold prices.

Retailer feedback highlights varying demand trends across store types. Large chain stores are witnessing a preference for lighter, lower-carat jewellery, while high-end independent stores catering to wedding customers are experiencing strong sales with minimal impact on volume. In contrast, smaller stores are struggling as footfall declines and sales fall.

Strong quarter for listed jewellers amid gold price surge

Despite the high base effect – caused by the July 2024 customs duty cut – and the current record-high gold prices (up ~43% y/y), listed jewellery retailers delivered a strong performance in the July-September quarter, with revenue growth ranging from 6.5% to 63%, according to their quarterly business updates.5

The early onset of the festive season (September vs. October last year) and strong wedding demand helped to partially offset the impact of last year’s high base. Targeted marketing campaigns, promotional initiatives, and customer-centric purchase schemes – such as old gold exchange offers, lightweight options and curated product launches – further supported consumer momentum. Retailers reported a notable uptick in demand for lighter, lower-carat jewellery as consumers adapted to higher prices. Meanwhile old gold exchange programs remained a significant sales driver across retailers.

Retail expansion also stayed strong, with leading players adding between five and 34 new stores during the quarter, including franchise and international formats, underscoring the sector's continued shift toward organised retail. Digital and e-commerce channels also saw significant traction, with some retailers reporting a y/y doubling of online revenue.

These large retailers remain upbeat about the current quarter, which is traditionally the strongest as it is driven by key festivals and peak wedding season demand.

ETFs: unprecedented inflows

Mirroring the global trend, Indian gold ETFs witnessed their largest-ever monthly net inflows in September, totalling INR83.6bn (US$947mn), a 282% increase m/m. Gold demand increased by 7.4t, closely aligned with our earlier estimate6 and marking the strongest month on record. The sustained and unprecedented gold price rally has captured investor interest, likely fuelling the surge in inflows; persistent safe-haven demand has also contributed. As per AMFI data,7 assets under management (AUM) in gold ETFs reached an all-time high of INR901.4bn (US$10.2bn), while cumulative holdings rose to 77.4t, a tenth of which was added in September alone.

Investor participation surged as well, with a record 629 thousand new accounts (folios) added in September. This took the total number of active folios to 8.66mn, a 33% increase since the start of the year.

Early data through October 10th indicates that this momentum is continuing, with strong net inflows of INR52.81bn (US$589mn) during this period.8

Investor profiles reveal notable differences in terms of AUM share and numbers of accounts. As per AMFI, retail investors accounted for 97% of total folios as of the end-June 2025 but only 8% of AUM, reflecting their smaller average ticket size. In contrast, corporates and high-net-worth individuals (HNIs) held a much larger share – 61% and 31% of AUM, respectively – indicating significantly higher investment per account. While the corporate share of total folios remained steady at 0.2%, HNIs showed rising participation, with their share of folios increasing from 1.4% in June 2024 to 3% by June 2025. This suggests growing interest in gold ETFs from affluent investors, possibly driven by portfolio diversification strategies.

Chart 3: Indian gold ETF holdings rise on record inflows

Monthly gold ETF flows in INRbn, and total holdings in tonnes*

In September 2025, based on our estimates using the RBI’s weekly data, the central bank added 0.2t to its gold reserves, bringing its total holdings to 880.2t. While the RBI's gold purchases in 2025 have been relatively muted – just 4t were added during the first nine months compared to 50t in the same period last year – the share of gold in India's foreign exchange reserves has increased significantly, rising from 9% to 14% over this period, largely driven by valuation gains from the rising gold price.

Gold imports rise sharply on seasonal and investment demand

India's gold imports surged to a ten-month high in September. Imports totalled US$9.16bn, marking a 77% m/m increase. The surge reflects seasonal buying ahead of the festival season, supported by robust investment demand. In volume terms, we estimate imports to be in the range of 100-104t, up from 65t in August.

Chart 4: Gold imports have been steadily rising

Monthly gold imports in tonnes and US$bn*

1Retail inflation slips to over 8-year low of 1.54% in September, NDTV, 14 October 2015.

2Based on LBMA Gold Price PM as of 15 October 2025.

3As of 14 October 2025.

4The share of bar and coin demand in India’s gold consumption (including jewellery and bars & coins) has increased from 29% in Q2’24 to 34% in Q2’25, Gold Demand Trends: Q2 2025, World Gold Council.

5Quarterly update – Titan Company, Kalyan Jewellers, PNG Jewellers, PC Jewellers Limited, Senco Gold Limited.

6WGC’s preliminary estimate, based on partial data, indicated net fund inflows of US$903mn and demand of 7.3t.

7As per the Association of Mutual Funds of India (AMFI) September 2025 report.

8Based on World Gold Council’s estimates of fund-wise net inflows from 1st to 10th October.

data.title: Nestlé: Solid Print Behind Layoff Headlines; Guidance Intact, Buy Maintained
data.description:
Nestlé’s (OTCPK:NSRGY) (OTCPK:NSRGF) (NEOE:NEST:CA) numbers hit this morning, and although layoff news dominated the headlines, we view the results as solid. Since our last update (Q2 results), Nestlé shares have gained 5.31% (Fig. 1); however, we are still down compared to our initiation of coverage. The company has recently transformed its portfolio, disposing of its US confectionery business and buying the rights to Starbucks products. For 2025, Nestlé strives to improve organic sales growth compared with 2024 and achieve an operating profit margin of at least 16%. Last November, during its capital market day, the company provided a clear turnaround plan and put a floor under the stock price. Although the CEO change was unexpected, there was no shift in strategy, and the new CEO has fully endorsed the strategic direction and action plan. Aside from a structural cost-saving initiative, Nestlé’s valuation does not look demanding, trading at a high discount to historical levels.

Fig 1

Q3 Results

As usual, Nestlé's Q3 print focused only on organic sales growth generation (OSG) and real internal growth (RIG). The company was up by 3.3% and 0.6%, respectively. The company managed to increase organic sales sequentially across all geographical regions. FX continues to weigh negatively on top-line sales performance. Looking at the GEO area, we report supportive sales in AOA (Zone Asia, Oceania, and Africa) with support in countries such as India, Pakistan, Indonesia, and Malaysia, while China continues to deteriorate. In the USA, we see softness in two categories: petcare and frozen food; however, we see coffee momentum coming back, and petcare product innovation will likely drive RIG.

Source: Nestlé's Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

The new CEO provided an overall constructive update. The leadership team reaffirmed 2025 and medium-term objectives and signaled no need to reset consensus expectations. Therefore, we believe Nestlé has reached a floor in terms of valuation downside risks. The CEO reiterated the ambition to lift the Underlying Operating Profit margin next year and to sustain 17%+ over the medium term (Fig. 3). So, Nestlé's growth framework is unchanged. It continues to target 4%+ organic growth through the cycle, including ≥2% RIG.

By category, confectionery and coffee were the largest organic growth contributors. This performance was pricing-led. As a reminder, in our last update, we addressed price elasticities and the confectionery was more pronounced in Q3, which was consistent with historical trends, but coffee was more resilient. On a positive note, organic growth was supportive across most categories. Consumer conditions remain choppy. Even so, management expects an uptick in underlying RIG into Q4; note that year-on-year comps and the timing of the Chinese New Year are technical headwinds. Despite this, they aim to deliver a positive Q4 RIG.

Froneri paid a €2.1 billion special dividend to Nestlé, reducing group net debt. This further reinforced our conviction in Nestlé’s fundamental strength within a high-quality portfolio. Free cash flow is expected to be above CHF 8 billion this year, with further improvement in 2026, even after today’s additional restructuring charges, driven by tighter capex and working capital management alongside margin expansion.

The company intends to reduce its global headcount by roughly 16,000 over the next two years. About 12,000 office roles across functions and regions are included, targeting CHF 1 billion in annual savings by end-2027 — double the prior CHF 500 million goal. One-off restructuring charges are expected at ~2x the yearly savings. An additional ~4,000 roles will be removed via ongoing manufacturing and supply chain productivity initiatives. The Fuel for Growth savings target rises to CHF 3.0 billion by end-2027 (from CHF 2.5 billion - Fig 4). While acknowledging the need to adapt cost structures, we do not favor layoffs as a primary driver of returns; we see this as a necessary step.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Changes And Valuation

Last time, Nestlé reported lower H2 margins vs H1 achievement of 16.5%. Therefore, we anticipate a gross margin decline due to FX, commodity cost inflation, and tariffs. We also accounted for lower advertising expenditure. The company is progressing well with the Fuel For Growth cost savings initiatives. With the latest update, the company confirmed a positive sequential improvement in RIG, and despite anticipating a core operating profit of CHF 14.9 billion, we are reassured about the medium-term company's outlook. The Q3 release provides no P&L disclosure, but Nestlé is still heavily discounted compared to its historical average. With the 13% share price appreciation after the Q3, the company reached our target price; however, in 2026, we are still not forecasting an EBIT margin above 17%. Rolling forward our forecast, with a conservative approach, we see Nestlé sales up by 3% with a core operating profit of 16.8%. The company's corporate tax rate is around 22%, and going down, we also include JV income for CHF 1.5 billion, which is offset by lower interest expense, now estimated at CHF 1.5 billion. Thanks to Froneri, the company slightly delivered and will reduce its interest expense payments. So, net income reached CHF 12.1 billion, and not taking into account the new buyback, and including the current number of shares, we derive an EPS of CHF 4.7. Applying an unchanged 20x P/E target, our target price is CHF 94 ($119 in ADR). While near-term upside appears capped, Nestlé has historically commanded a premium multiple (≈22–25x), and we don’t think the turnaround is fully reflected. We maintain a Buy rating.

Risks

Nestlé downside risks include: 1) consumer slowdown with change in taste, 2) given the elevated inflation, the company risks is higher price elasticity with inability to increase price (this also has repercussions in consumers shifting to lower quality products), 3) FX changes with impact on the company’s P&L (this is the main risk), 4) continuous changes in the leadership team, 5) lower than anticipated cost saving initiatives, 6) tariffs risks, 7) higher restructuring costs, 8) lower performance in marketing, 9) (NEW) bad press from ongoing headcount reduction.

Conclusion

In our last update, the company traded at a 16.2x P/E, and we view the derating as exaggerated. Nestlé was and is still the world's largest food and beverage player. We continue to like its equity story, given that the company remains a buy on fundamental value, but there is additional upside to price in.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway Energy: Quiet Beneficiary Of The AI Power Boom
data.description:
Since my last Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) analysis, the stock has lost -2.36% in price.

Demand for electricity is surging as artificial intelligence drives an explosion in data centers and high-performance computing. Meanwhile, Berkshire Hathaway Energy ("BHE") is sitting at the intersection of the demand curve by providing the critical power infrastructure needed to run energy-intensive GPUs and server farms.

AI data centers are intensely power-hungry; U.S. AI computing demand is projected to grow by 30x to 123 GW by 2035, and hyperscale facilities under development will draw up to 2 GW each (essentially rivaling big power plants). BHE operates in key data center locations, like Nevada and Iowa, and BHE's utilities are seeing unprecedented load growth from data center customers. BHE's NV Energy (Nevada) report tech firms requesting 21–22 GW of new supply, effectively tripling its 9 GW load.

BHE is currently a $26+ billion revenue business, with attributable net earnings of over $4 billion as of the last full financial year. As the company is wholly owned by Berkshire Hathaway, 100% of BHE's earnings are available for reinvestment, this allows the company to focus on scaling for AI-era needs easier than companies that need to tap external equity as they have distribution obligations for a diversified cohort of shareholders. Berkshire's recent purchase of BHE's remaining minority stake valued the business at ~$49 billion, which is about 1x book value. Peer utilities commonly trade around 2x book value, so the valuation was more than favorable for Berkshire's shareholders. If BHE successfully capitalizes on AI-driven growth, its earnings power and intrinsic value could increase substantially, and Berkshire's earlier decision to wholly control BHE will seem doubly wise in retrospect.

Energy Companies Are The Constant Winners Of The AI Boom

Today, the largest data center complexes draw ~300–500 MW each, but next-generation AI campuses are planning for 1–2 GW of load. Mega-sites are also in early development now, with 50,000-acre "AI hubs" that could consume 5 GW at a single location, which is more than a large nuclear plant. This is already straining local grids; data centers concentrate 24/7 high loads in one spot, causing unusual grid challenges like voltage distortions and potential outages. Therefore, building more energy capacity is undoubtedly not only useful, but structurally necessary. You can see why the geography of AI infrastructure is broadening; while Northern Virginia and Silicon Valley have traditionally been data center hubs, power and land constraints there are pushing hyperscalers into regions with cheaper, cleaner power. This is playing directly into BHE's footprint as states like Nevada, Iowa, Utah, and Oregon (all served by BHE subsidiaries) get flooded with data center investment.

Iowa's MidAmerican Energy is also seeing a data center haven emerge around it. Iowa now hosts 64 data centers (including Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Microsoft (MSFT) facilities). The reason these players have honed in on Iowa is that MidAmerican offers electricity rates 42% below the national average. The company has also filed plans for 800 MW of new solar plus 465 MW of gas peaking capacity to ensure round-the-clock supply. Its long-term resource study projects above-average load growth from contracted large users (i.e. data centers) and flags a capacity shortfall by 2026 without new builds. Iowa policymakers have even been exploring opening an idle 600 MW nuclear plant and enabling small modular reactors, specifically citing data centers' vast electricity thirst as a catalyst.

In the West, BHE's PacifiCorp is planning massive renewables and transmission expansions to serve new industrial and tech loads. PacifiCorp serves parts of Utah, Oregon, Washington, Wyoming, and Idaho. However, PacifiCorp's aging grid equipment was implicated in catastrophic wildfires in 2020, leading to lawsuits. BHE Renewables has opportunities to develop dedicated solar/wind farms for hyperscalers under long-term contracts across the U.S. BHE's Northern Powergrid subsidiary in the UK could also see incremental AI-related load around cities like Leeds and Newcastle, but the AI boom is currently most pronounced in the U.S.

Fundamental Upside Catalysts

We should state the obvious here, which is that BHE will directly profit from the AI boom by selling more electricity. Data centers run at high capacity utilization 24/7, so each new facility that can consume will translate into higher gigawatt-hour sales. As data centers already account for 15%+ of total load in some regions, as AI drives that higher, BHE's top-line will logically rise accordingly. A virtuous cycle of growth will also emerge to the extent that BHE's territories gain competitive advantages with cheap renewable power and available land.

The AI boom is essentially handing BHE an opportunity to deploy massive capital into new capacity (including transmission lines, substations, wind/solar farms, gas backup plants, and grid hardening), with the confidence that demand will be there. Regulators are generally supportive if investments are tied to serving new industry load and maintaining reliability; for example, Nevada regulators approved the $4.2 billion Greenlink transmission build and renewables because of high data center load and renewable integration needs. Iowa lawmakers are also easing ratemaking to facilitate generation additions for large customers.

There are also pricing and tariff innovations benefiting BHE. As a key example, NV Energy has introduced a special tariff allowing data centers to buy renewable energy, and even invest in generation, for an extra fee. Furthermore, when the grid is constrained, utilities may levy capacity charges or require financial contributions from new large users. In essence, sometimes, scarcity of immediate power can lead to higher prices in open markets, but most of BHE's markets are regulated, not merchant. But even regulated utilities periodically file rate cases, as surging demand can justify rate increases to fund reliability upgrades. I really do believe that one of the biggest caps to energy renaissance will be regulatory hesitation or complacency, so the U.S. government will likely need deregulation to be front-and-center in the energy arena to win the AI race.

Valuation

Berkshire bought the last 8% of BHE at a price implying ~$49 billion total value, which was roughly 1x book value ($50 billion).

The bear case is that even with AI growth, BHE's valuation shouldn't be much higher because it's fundamentally a regulated utility with ~10% ROE cap, unlike tech companies that can scale profits exponentially. Realistically, BHE will steadily create value but not deliver incredible alpha in terms of ROI, given the capital intensity of projects and earnings growth likely in the mid-to-high single-digits. The contribution to Berkshire's valuation is also not a dramatic upside swing. Nonetheless, the energy thesis is resilient right now, and at very least, BHE will provide capital security to Berkshire's diversified, fundamentally-oriented portfolio.

At the implied ~$49 billion valuation, BHE constitutes approximately 5% of Berkshire's market cap (around $1T). It's very plausible for the market to start re-rating BHE as the AI thesis compounds over the next decade. BHE may start to incrementally deserve a higher multiple like peers (e.g. 1.8x book given its renewable growth profile and structural resiliency across multiple core data center regions). That would make its value more like $80–90 billion, adding $30–40 billion to Berkshire's sum-of-parts over what the purchase price implied. Also, BHE's retained earnings will compound within Berkshire. I would be interested in owning shares in BHE if it was spun-off and hope to see this happen in the Abel-as-Berkshire-CEO era.

Risks

Honing in on the regulatory risk, if public utility commissions fear that residential customers will subsidize Big Tech's power needs, there could be push back on projects or returns. For example, regulators halved NV Energy's proposed rate increase for new transmission. In Iowa, there's concern that ratepayers not foot the bill for data center-driven expansion. There's some logic that AI companies should bear the cost, which would reduce BHE's profit opportunity.

There's also a risk that BHE overbuilds capacity, expecting demand that then doesn't materialize on time. Efficiency gains in chips or a shift to distributed computing could slow the growth in power needs. Prudency reviews might then disallow recovery on unused investments.

Utility infrastructure, especially in the U.S. West, carries operational risks that can lead to large financial hits (consider the lawsuits from the 2020 Oregon wildfires). Limiting wildfire risk is now a top priority for BHE and it comes with increased operating and capital costs that could strain margins.

If natural gas prices spike, electricity prices would rise, and regulators may delay other increases or push for rate reductions in BHE's domains. Also, distributed energy (solar + battery) could cut into utility sales if large customers choose to self-generate. If AI firms start bypassing utilities, BHE's growth could be more moderate in the long term than the bull case would assume. But given the current scale of demand there's limited near-term risk.

Conclusion: Buy

Berkshire Hathaway Energy is emerging as one of the clearest, least-recognized beneficiaries of the AI boom as hyperscale data centers and GPU clusters drive unprecedented electricity demand. BHE has developed a regulated footprint across Nevada, Iowa, Utah, and Oregon, which are all key U.S. tech regions and make the company positioned formidably in the growth path.

Berkshire now has full ownership of BHE, giving it capital flexibility and allowing it to deploy billions into new generation, transmission, and renewable assets without shareholder dilution; this will allow BHE to capture strong and durable returns.

That said, growth will be steady rather than exponential, but BHE offers structural resilience, inflation-linked earnings, and exposure to one of the century's most critical bottlenecks. If you view Berkshire as a sum of the parts, BHE is a defensive compounder and a strategic lever for AI-era value creation. Buy.

data.title: China Gold Market Update: Wholesale Demand Rebounded
data.description:
Highlights

  • Gold continues to shine. The LBMA Gold Price PM in USD and the Shanghai Benchmark Gold Price PM (SHAUPM) in RMB both soared by 12% last month, extending y-t-d gains to levels unseen for decades
  • Gold withdrawals from the Shanghai Gold Exchange (SGE) reached 118t in September, 33t higher m/m and 4t higher y/y; Q3 ended with a total withdrawal of 297t, 9t lower y/y
  • Chinese gold ETFs saw inflows in September (RMB4.5bn, US$622mn), narrowing their Q3 losses, and gold futures activity on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) improved notably
  • The People’s Bank of China (PBoC) reported its 11th consecutive monthly gold purchase, adding 1.2t in September and pushing the Q3 total to 5t.

Looking ahead

  • After re-stocking in September, gold jewellery retailers will likely dial back their replenishment in October, following seasonal trends. Furthermore, the gold price rally may continue to weigh on gold jewellery consumption, adding to retailer caution.
  • Investment demand for gold may be buoyed by the attractive gold price rally, particularly when the local equity momentum took a hit amid flaring US-China tension.

Chart 1: The SHAUPM saw its strongest Q1~Q3 performance since its launch in 2016

Cumulative return of the SHAUPM in RMB between Q1 and Q3*

Another record-shattering month

Gold’s strength continued in September. The LBMA Gold Price PM recorded its largest monthly increase since January 2012 and its strongest quarterly performance since Q1 2016. But more notably, the SHAUPM registered its strongest month on record and its second-strongest quarter ever with a 14% gain—just behind Q1 2025’s 18% surge.

Having reached record highs 36 times during the first three quarters of 2025, the SHAUPM’s y-t-d return extended to 36%, to date the best year on record (Chart 1). The LBMA Gold Price PM jumped 43% between January and September, the best y-t-d performance since 1979. Gold’s relatively weaker return in China was mainly due to a 4% appreciation in the RMB against the dollar during the period.

It is also worth noting that the gold price has carried its bullish momentum into October, with the LBMA Gold Price PM breaching the US$4,000/oz threshold for the first time ever on 8 October and the SHAUPM surging nearly 5% during the month’s first trading day.1

Wholesale demand ended Q3 with an improving September

Wholesale gold demand saw a 33t m/m rebound in September, reaching 118t; 4t higher y/y (Chart 2). Nonetheless, it remained 33% below the long-term average. Our interactions with market participants inform us that as the gold price resumed its uptrend, bar and coin sales improved during the month, supporting wholesale demand. But some investors took the opportunity to book profits by selling their holdings.

September usually brings a seasonal uplift from manufacturers’ new product launches and retailers’ pre-Golden Week holiday restocking. Yet this year, soaring gold prices and doubts about future sales have tempered retailers' restocking enthusiasm.

Chart 2: Wholesale gold demand improved in September

Monthly gold withdrawals from the SGE*

Chart 3: Gold withdrawals from the SGE remained weak in Q3

Q3 gold withdrawals from the SGE*

Chinese gold ETFs finally saw inflows in September, following two consecutive monthly losses (Chart 4). Attracted by the strong gold price performance, Chinese investors bought RMB4.5bn (US$622mn) of local gold ETFs. September’s inflows and the surging price lifted Chinese gold ETFs’ total AUM to another month-end peak of RMB169bn (US$22bn), with holdings rebounding by 4.9t to 194t.

Chart 4: Chinese gold ETFs saw inflows in September

Monthly Chinese gold ETF flows and month-end AUM*

But September’s inflows were not sufficient to reverse earlier losses, resulting in Q3 net outflows of RMB3.8bn (US$537mn) (Chart 5) and collective holdings reduced by 5.8t during the period. As previously noted, Chinese investor risk appetite improved as the need for safe-haven assets declined: July and August saw better growth expectations on the back of strong Q2 economic data and a reducing US-China tariff risk. This sentiment was reflected in the strong CSI300 Stock Index rally, which surged 18% during Q3, the strongest quarter since Q1 2019. Meanwhile, rising government bond yields – as the market priced in fewer rate cuts by the PBoC – increased opportunity costs of holding gold.

Nonetheless, helped by various uncertainties, the strong price performance, a lack of alternative investment options, and encouragement from the central bank’s non-stop gold buying, Chinese gold ETF inflows during the first three quarters remain the largest ever. In fact, the cumulative inflows of RMB59bn (US$8.2bn, 79t) between Q1 and Q3 have surpassed all previous annual records (Chart 5).

Chart 5: Q3 outflows have little impact on y-t-d strength

Quarterly gold ETF tonnage demand*

Chart 6: Trading volumes of gold futures rebounded in September

Daily average trading volumes of SHFE gold futures and monthly gold price volatility*

The PBoC reported its 11th consecutive monthly gold purchase

In September, the PBoC announced a 1.2t gold purchase, its 11th non-stop addition. By the end of Q3 China’s official gold holdings stood at 2,303t, 7.7% of total foreign reserves.

China’s gold reserves increased by 5t, a slower pace than in previous quarters (Chart 7). This is perhaps not surprising: as the gold price surges, central banks may dial back the amount of gold buying for tactical reasons. Yet judging from the PBoC’s four-quarter non-stop gold purchases, we believe gold remains a key strategic asset to central banks looking to diversify assets in a fragmented and uncertain world.

Chart 7: Four quarters of non-stop PBoC gold purchasing announcements

Reported official gold holdings and the quarterly average LBMA Gold Price PM*

China imported a net 87t of gold in August, based on the latest information, a mild 2t m/m fall (Chart 8). Imports remained broadly stable as the average local gold price premium fell only slightly in the month (US$7.6/oz in August vs US$8/oz in July); the upcoming peak season for gold also limited the m/m weakness.

Chart 8: August gold imports remained relatively stable

Net 7108 gold imports under various regimes*

1Chinese markets were closed between 1 and 8 October due to the National Day Holiday and the Mid-Autumn Day.

Original Post

data.title: 3 Steps For Active EM Bond Investing
data.description:
Emerging markets bonds offer high yields, low debt, and policy-driven resilience – this is our three-step process for capturing alpha in the space.

Emerging markets (EM) bonds have grown into one of the most compelling opportunities in fixed income. With lower debt levels, more independent central banks, and attractive real yields, many EMs are better positioned than developed markets to deliver stability and growth. Yet, EM debt remains under-owned, in part because outcomes can diverge dramatically between countries and currencies. Below, we summarize the investment process for the actively managed VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF, which aims to capitalize on these disparities and opportunities across the EM debt spectrum.

A Valuation-Based Investment Process

At the core of our EM bond strategy is a simple principle: buy cheap bonds, not good or bad risks. To do that, step 1 of our investment process builds a radar chart showing each country’s superiority, or inferiority, relative to the global average measured in units of standard deviation across a range of 16 fundamental metrics. These metrics include:

  • Standard measures such as general government debt/GDP
  • Flow measures such as the current account deficit/GDP
  • Structural measures, such as the banking system’s common equity-to-assets ratio

We display these as a radar chart, as shown below. When the country result is inside the global mean result, it is superior to the global mean. Conversely, when the result is outside the global mean, it is inferior to the global average.

Brazil’s Radar Chart Measuring Fundamental Risk

From there, we then “line up” all the radar charts for the countries and companies in the investable universe. We do this by calculating a z-score (a way of measuring how far a data point is from the average of a dataset) based on each country’s superiority, or inferiority, to the global mean, as measured by the metrics detailed in the chart. We compare these z-scores and bonds with similar yields. You can see the fundamental z-score on the x-axis in the chart below. Those with superior fundamentals are on the left, inferior to the right. We highlight Brazil and see that the real yields (y-axis) of Brazil’s 10-year local-currency bonds are higher than the predicted level (along the dotted line), based on all the other EM 10-year local-currency bonds. They are therefore considered cheap. We conduct this exercise for every tenor of bond. And we conduct it for hard-currency bonds using yield or spread on the y-axis, which you can see in the chart below.

In this chart, you can see that Brazilian hard-currency bonds are expensive. The output of Step 1 in our process is a list of the cheapest bonds in EM, whether local, hard, sovereign, or corporate, based entirely on their deviation from the predicted level. The cheaper they are, the more they are liked and therefore the higher they are on the list. You may notice that Step 1 favors tenors/duration based entirely on cheapness relative to fundamentals, not the investment teams’ top-down opinion on U.S. ‘duration.’ If the 20-year Brazilian local currency bond was cheaper relative to the predicted level than the 10-year Brazilian local currency bond, the 20-year bond is higher in the ranking.

Real 10-year Local Currency Valuations and Country Fundamentals Determine which 10-year Local Currency Bonds Pay Real Yields that are Relatively Too High

Sovereign 5-year Bond Valuations and Country Fundamentals Determine which 5-year Hard Currency Sovereign Bonds Pay Yields that Are Relatively Too High

The result of Step 1 is a list of the cheapest bonds in EM, without reference to whether they are local currency, hard-currency sovereign, or hard-currency corporate. It is an entirely quantitative process. In EM, however, there are many non-systematic risks, such as politics, elections, index exclusions/inclusions, global trade partnerships and treaties that are not appropriately managed by the quantitative framework in Step 1.

This is why we have Step 2, which may eliminate or impact the ranking of bonds chosen by Step 1. Step 2 of the process involves three ‘tests’:

  1. Political/policy
  2. Economic
  3. Technical A ‘strong fail’ result on any of these removes a bond from the list.

Each test is applied bond-by-bond, so the investment team can fail a 10-year local currency bond from a country but not a 5-year hard-currency bond from the same country. All changes in Step 2 are subjective and documented by the investment team monthly.

Step 3 is portfolio construction. In Step 1, the cheaper a bond is relative to the predicted level, the greater the allocation. If a bond is in the top quartile of cheapness, it gets the maximum allocation of 1.5 times the benchmark weight for that country. If a bond is in the second quartile, the maximum allocation is 1.25 times the benchmark weight for that country and so on, until the portfolio is full. The portfolio is constructed in consideration of risk constraints.

VanEck Emerging Markets Bond Strategy

The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF was one of the first blended emerging markets bond strategies in the market. The Strategy adopts a comprehensive approach, investing across the entire EM bond spectrum to maximize opportunity and manage risk in a complex global environment. Despite global disruptions such as the COVID pandemic, the war in Ukraine and economic troubles in China, the fund has consistently outperformed both global and U.S. bond benchmarks, earning top-quartile rankings from Morningstar over the past five years. This outperformance, both absolute and risk-adjusted, is also evident when compared to the fund’s benchmark, which includes the same EM bonds but without active management. VanEck’s active strategy, which focuses on fundamental value relative to bond risk premia, aims to capitalize on these shifts and avoid troubled issuers, making a compelling case for a diversified, actively managed EM bond allocation.
